item.title: This 10-port docking station dropped from $150 to $25, making it the perfect time to upgrade your workspace
item.itunes:title: This 10-port docking station dropped from $150 to $25, making it the perfect time to upgrade your workspace
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
If you're someone who uses a laptop and is looking to expand your setup, adding a docking station is a no-brainer. You get an assortment of ports, dedicated power, and all at a price that's not going to hurt your wallet.

This particular model is from Brydge Stone and features all the essential ports you'll need. And while it was introduced at a price of $150, it can now be had for a fraction of the price from Woot at just $25.

Plus, at this price, it's a good way to give something new a try. So, just in case it doesn't work out for you, you aren't spending a ton to experiment.

What's great about this 10-in-1 docking station?

Port selection is probably going to be the most crucial when it comes to choosing a docking station. And this one has a pretty good selection, with two USB-C, four USB-A, HDMI, DisplayPort, SD card reader, and Ethernet.

When it comes to the port details, one of the USB-C ports can supply up to 100W, which is great for powering devices like laptops, tablets, phones, and other accessories. You also get HDMI output at 8K at 30Hz, and the DisplayPort can output at 4K at 60Hz.

And since this docking station is quite compact, the desk might look a bit less cluttered when in use. For the most part, you can't really go wrong here. This docking station has everything you need to expand your devices, and comes in at a price that's easy to afford.

Brydge Stone CORE USB-C MST Docking Station
$25 $40 Save $15
Ports
2x USB-C, 4x USB-A, HDMI 2.1 (8K), DisplayPort 1.4 (4K), SD 4.0 card reader, and Gigabit Ethernet
USB Power Delivery
Yes
Power supply included
Yes
Warranty
Yes
Max display res.
4K
$25 at Woot

item.title: This Alienware deal proves you don't have to overspend on gaming monitors
item.itunes:title: This Alienware deal proves you don't have to overspend on gaming monitors
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
There are plenty of great monitors to choose from, but this one from Alienware seems to get everything right if you're looking for great performance at an affordable price. This 27-inch gaming monitor features a 240Hz refresh rate, 1ms response time, and support for technologies like Nvidia's G-Sync and VESA's Adaptive Sync certification.

What's great about the Alienware AW2723DF gaming monitor?

You get great colors and contrast, along with a reliable panel that will deliver day in and day out. The best part about this monitor right now is that you can grab it for a great price, coming in well below its original retail price. Amazon's got it for $323, which is the lowest price that we've ever seen. Just be sure to act fast to save.

In addition to the above, you also get customizable LED lighting on the rear, a built-in hanger for your headset, and plenty of connectivity. The monitor features two HDMI, one DisplayPort, four USB-A, a 3.5mm audio jack, and a USB-B port. There are also customization options built into the software to optimize the experience.

This monitor can handle all that you can throw at it, and can even become a dedicated media hub when you connect a streaming stick and gaming consoles. For the most part, it's everything you need at a price that's easy to afford. Just be sure to act quickly because a price like this won't last long.

Alienware AW2723DF Gaming Monitor
$323 $427 Save $104

Dell's Alienware AW2723DF is a higher-end 1440p gaming monitor that comes with a 240Hz refresh rate and has FreeSync Premium Pro anti-screen tearing technology that'll work for any modern GPU.

$323 at Amazon

item.title: I built a clipboard server on the Arduino Uno Q, and it replaced a workflow I didn't realize I hated
item.itunes:title: I built a clipboard server on the Arduino Uno Q, and it replaced a workflow I didn't realize I hated
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
If you work across multiple devices, you've almost certainly emailed yourself a link, pasted something into a notes app just to open it on another machine, or maybe even typed out a URL by hand because you couldn't be bothered to find a better way. Clipboard sync between devices is a largely solved problem by now, but sometimes it's nice to just have a simple way to quickly and easily copy text between two devices without any additional software. Sure, Apple has Universal Clipboard, Windows has its own clipboard sync, and third-party tools like ClipCascade let you self-host something, but none of them felt right for the level of simplicity that I wanted

I've been spending a lot of time with the Arduino Uno Q just to have a bit of fun. I've talked about what makes it such a weird and interesting board, and I also turned it into a Bluetooth streaming server for my speakers. This time, I wanted to try something more practical: a network-attached clipboard that any device on my local network could push to and pull from, no cloud involved.

The result is a small Flask app I'm calling ClipDrop, and it ended up being one of the most genuinely useful things I've built on the Uno Q so far. It's basic and simple, but it does exactly what I want it to.

The Uno Q is genuinely good at this kind of thing

Its Bridge architecture is genuinely unique

The Arduino Uno Q's dual-processor setup is what makes a project like this possible in the first place. The Qualcomm QRB2210 side runs Debian Linux, so you can run a full Python web server on it. The STM32U585 microcontroller handles real-time tasks and has direct access to the onboard hardware, including the 8x13 LED matrix. The two sides talk to each other through what Arduino calls the Bridge, and that's what holds ClipDrop together.

Arduino's official way to build and deploy apps to the Uno Q is through App Lab, a rather simple IDE that handles both the Python and C++ sides. It works, and for a lot of projects it's fine, but plugging the Uno Q into a dock and using it with a display is slow. The whole desktop experience is quite painful, which makes sense, as Arduino recommends using the 4GB RAM variant for desktop usage. As a result, I ended up doing most of the development and deployment over SSH instead. I just use SCP to copy the project folder onto the board, SSH in, and use the arduino-app-cli to start and stop the app from the terminal. It's faster, more predictable, and honestly just more comfortable if you're used to working in a terminal anyway.

The project structure itself follows the same pattern as Arduino's own example apps. If you look at the official examples on GitHub, like the blink demo in Arduino's app-bricks-examples repo, you'll see the same layout: an app.yaml manifest, a python/ folder with a main.py for the Linux side, and a sketch/ folder with a .ino file for the STM32. ClipDrop follows that structure exactly, just with a Flask server and a web UI bolted on top of it. Having that template to work from made the initial setup a lot easier, because the Bridge wiring between Python and the sketch is quite an abstract concept.

ClipDrop is a Flask server that serves a single-page web app. You open it in a browser on any device connected to your network, and you get a text box and a file drop zone. Paste some text, hit "Push," and it's stored on the Uno Q. Pick up your phone, open the same page, and the text is there. You can also drag and drop files up to 50 MB, download them from another device, and delete them when you're done. It's not doing anything particularly clever on the software side, and that's kind of the point. The Flask server handles the API, a single HTML file handles the UI, and the Bridge connects it all to the STM32 for a bit of physical feedback.

That physical feedback is my favorite part of the whole thing. Whenever you push text to the clipboard, the LED matrix flashes a "C" for three seconds. Upload a file, and it flashes an "F" instead. It turns what would otherwise be a completely invisible network service into something you can actually see working. The STM32 sketch defines those letter shapes as bit arrays and uses the Bridge to listen for calls from the Python side. When the Flask server receives new data, it calls a function to flash the clipboard or file image. It's simple, but it's neat to see on my desk when I'm using it.

It replaced a workflow I didn't realize I hated

And it's not trying to be something it isn't

I didn't build ClipDrop expecting it to become part of my daily routine, but here we are. I'll copy a URL on my laptop, push it to ClipDrop, and pull it on my phone when I'm heading out. I'll find an interesting piece of information on my phone while reading something online, push it to ClipDrop, and pull it up on my desktop when I sit back down. The file transfer is handy too, especially for moving screenshots or small documents between computers without hunting for a cable or opening yet another app. PairDrop somewhat works for this, but this tool removes any barriers that remained.

Of course, in a production context, this isn't necessarily the most useful application. There's no authentication, so anyone on your network can read and write to it. The text clipboard lives in memory and resets when the app restarts, so it's not persistent storage. Also, there's no encryption, no versioning, and no conflict resolution. If two people push text at the same time, the last one wins.

Join the newsletter for Uno Q projects and ClipDrop tips

Subscribe to the newsletter to get more Uno Q projects and ClipDrop-style how-tos with code snippets, Bridge wiring tips, and practical setup notes for building simple local clipboard and file-transfer tools. Ideal for makers seeking reproducible microcon
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

For my use case, none of that matters. This runs on my home network behind my router's firewall. I'm the only one using it, and I don't need clipboard history or encrypted channels for the kinds of things I'm copying; links, text snippets, the occasional config file, and the like. If you wanted to make it more secure, you could add basic authentication to the Flask routes or put it behind a reverse proxy, but that felt like overengineering for a clipboard on a microcontroller.

The 50 MB file size limit is there as a safety net for the Uno Q's limited storage, but in practice, I've never come close to hitting it. Most of what I transfer is text or small files. If you need to move large files between machines, there are better tools for that. ClipDrop is for the small everyday transfers that you'd otherwise solve by emailing yourself or dropping a link in a chat app just so you can tap it on another device. This is also what makes it more powerful than an ESP32 used for the same purpose: for most ESP32 devices, you can't store a 50MB file.

I started ClipDrop off as a silly project to get some use out of the Arduino Uno Q, but I've actually left it running over the past week, and I've been using it more than I thought I would. Is it a niche use case? For sure, but I'm getting some surprising value out of it. If you want to give it a try, I've published it over on GitHub.

ClipDrop is for the small everyday transfers that you'd otherwise solve by emailing yourself or dropping a link in a chat app just so you can tap it on another device. This is also what makes it more powerful than an ESP32 used for the same purpose: for most ESP32 devices, you can't store a 50MB file.

I started ClipDrop off as a silly project to get some use out of the Arduino Uno Q, but I've actually left it running over the past week, and I've been using it more than I thought I would. Is it a niche use case? For sure, but I'm getting some surprising value out of it. If you want to give it a try, I've published it over on GitHub.

item.title: CPU bottlenecks are holding back modern GPUs more than you think
item.itunes:title: CPU bottlenecks are holding back modern GPUs more than you think
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
If you're the kind of person who spends well over $1,000 on high-end GPUs, you probably expect a massive jump in performance across every game you play after each upgrade. I mean, that's the whole point of splurging on something like the RTX 4090 or 5090, isn't it? You're not just chasing higher frame rates; you're also expecting your games to feel smoother and more responsive, especially if you're gaming on a 240Hz or 360Hz monitor. But if you've ever upgraded your GPU only to be underwhelmed by the FPS uplift, you're not alone.

I face this issue myself as someone who has paired the RTX 4090 with a 5800X3D. And before you jump to conclusions, this CPU was one of the best options on the market when Nvidia launched the card in 2022. Sure, at 4K, this isn't a big deal unless I'm playing competitive titles, but at 1440p, the CPU limitations are impossible to overlook. Likewise, you could pair a 9800X3D with the RTX 5090 and still have your GPU underutilized more often than you'd expect. And that's what I want to talk about here.

Don't buy a high-end GPU unless you have these parts dialed in

You can't cut corners if you're spending over $1,000 on a GPU.

By  Hamlin Rozario

High-end GPUs get CPU-limited even at 1440p

Your CPU is the reason your 240Hz or 360Hz monitor isn't fully utilized

As much as we like to think that flagship GPUs like the RTX 4090 and 5090 are for 4K gaming, many competitive gamers do pair them with 1440p ultra-high refresh rate monitors. On paper, that makes sense because you've got more than enough GPU power to easily push past 200FPS to have an edge in fast-paced titles. But this is exactly where CPU limitations kick in, and your GPU ends up being underutilized. Even if you have the fastest CPU available, you'll see your GPU usage often dip well below 90% at these frame rates.

Chasing flagship GPUs for 1440p gaming isn't really worth your money, even if you have a CPU like the 9800X3D. Take a look at the RTX 5090, for example. According to benchmarks, it's only about 12% faster on average than the RTX 4090 at 1440p. That's nowhere near the 27% jump you get at 4K. And this gap is a result of CPU limitations, so all that extra GPU horsepower just doesn't translate into real-world performance gains. You're essentially paying for performance that your current CPU can't unlock.

Even at 4K, competitive games still expose CPU limits

The RTX 5090 shines in AAA games, but CPU-bound titles tell a different story

There's no doubt that most modern games tax GPUs so hard at 4K that you'll need a high-end card like the RTX 4090 or 5090 to maintain playable frame rates unless you want to rely on upscaling or frame generation. In those situations, your GPU is doing exactly what you paid for, which is pushing visuals as far as possible while holding steady performance. But that only really applies to AAA titles that are naturally GPU-intensive, so your CPU doesn't matter as much. You can even get away with using a CPU from a couple of generations ago and still get similar results.

That completely changes in competitive games, even at 4K. The moment you start chasing higher frame rates instead of visual fidelity, your CPU becomes far more important than most people expect. These games are designed to run at 200FPS or more, which means your GPU often has plenty of headroom while your CPU struggles to keep up. When I play competitive titles like Valorant and Counter-Strike: 2, my RTX 4090's usage rarely exceeds 80% at native 4K when paired with the 5800X3D, which tells you everything you need to know about where the bottleneck actually is.

Most gamers aren't chasing very high frame rates

But for those who do, even the best CPUs aren't really enough

You could argue that most gamers aren't playing at 200+FPS, and I totally get that. The vast majority of people who get the RTX 4090 or 5090 mostly play AAA games at 4K, where the GPU is doing most of the heavy lifting. In those games, you're rarely getting over 100FPS unless you have upscaling or frame generation enabled anyway, so even a CPU that's a few years old isn't going to noticeably affect its performance.

However, there are people who enjoy high refresh rate gaming just as much as I do. After all, 240Hz and 360Hz OLEDs are popular these days for a reason. The moment you step into that territory, you're not limited by how fast your GPU can render frames, but by how quickly your CPU can keep up. Sure, you could get the fastest CPU available today to minimize potential bottlenecks, but even then, you’ll still run into scenarios where your GPU isn’t fully utilized. At that point, you're just sitting there wondering why you splurged on a 1440p/360Hz monitor and a flagship GPU when your CPU is holding everything back.

CPUs need to catch up so GPUs can flex without relying on 4K

We've reached a point where throwing more GPU horsepower at a game doesn't automatically translate into a better gaming experience, especially if you’re not playing at 4K. At 1440p and high refresh rates, the CPU decides how much of that performance you actually get to use. That's why CPUs need to catch up, not just in raw performance, but in how well they can keep flagship GPUs fully utilized across different gaming scenarios. Until that happens, I don't think even an RTX 6090 would help gamers get the most out of their ultra-high refresh rate monitors.

Your expensive CPU was a waste of money — here's why

Your high-end CPU is a mere spectator inside your PC

By  Tanveer Singh

item.title: I’m planning a trip to Paris and this self-hosted app has been a game changer
item.itunes:title: I’m planning a trip to Paris and this self-hosted app has been a game changer
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
I'd like to say I've traveled extensively with over a hundred countries under my belt. And the one piece of advice I give to everyone is, don't overcomplicate travel. You might want to bookmark a few must-visit locations, perhaps have a rough itinerary for the day, but I encourage everyone to figure it out on the go. But there are a few countries that break that norm. I've been planning a short getaway to Paris, and despite this being my fifth trip over, I can confirm that there's just way too much packed in that city. Every arrondissement has something interesting to see, and every time I'm over, I'm left juggling maps, pins, screenshots, and notes with no real structure to them. Let's just say that it's a problem.

That's why, while looking for a better solution to the problem, I stumbled upon Trip. This self-hosted travel planner focuses on helping you map out the places you want to visit. But more importantly, it helps you create a cohesive itinerary based on your data. It's not jam-packed with features to the point that it would confuse you on the go. Instead, it offers a clean way to organize all your travel data in one place, and it's completely changed how I look at travel planning.

I still use Waze over Google Maps because its community reporting features are unmatched

Crowdsourced hazard reporting is the map app's killer feature.

9
By  Patrick O'Rourke

Trip planning with a map-first approach

Bridge together your scattered research into one cohesive view

Unlike more conventional approaches to travel planning that invariably revolve around lists, Trip focuses on the map. Every place I come across during my research is added to Trip as a point on the map. This includes things like interesting restaurants, museums, cafés, and even random spots that I might've stumbled across on Reddit. Photo spots, in particular, are my jam, and I'm always on the lookout for options.

That fixes a fairly big problem in my previous approach. While my map pins were on Google Maps, notes were in Google Keep, and I'd usually have a browser full of tabs open just in case. That's pretty disjointed. Trip brings all these disjointed data points together and anchors them to physical locations on a map.

Since the goal of the app is to manage all these data points, you're always aware of how they connect to each other. That gives you a glanceable view of what you're planning and how far these places might be from each other. It plays a big role in the day-to-day itinerary-making process.

For example, if I drop in a bunch of places to visit clustered around the same area, I know I've got a full day's itinerary chalked out. Meanwhile, if I can quite clearly see that things are far apart, something needs to change. I'm not accidentally planning out a trip where places are so far apart that I end up spending most of the day in transit.

There's a deep layer of organization built in, too. For example, I can group places, filter them, and even add context with personal notes like how long I expect to spend at a spot. A museum like the Louvre? Better chalk out an entire afternoon for that. All this data sits on your own home server, which is a massive benefit for avid travelers like me. Over the years, I've used a lot of travel mapping apps, and many of those no longer exist. That also means that my personal itineraries are no longer accessible. If I want to pass on that information along, I have to scour through photos to put the pieces back together. With Trip, that's no longer a stress point. It even lets you print out a cleaned-up version of your itinerary.

Turn saved places into a plan that you can follow

Map out your days, not just locations

As I mentioned earlier, saving data points is the easy part. The real complexity arises when you are trying to build a travel plan that you can follow through on when you're on the ground. That's where Trip gets really good. Once I've mapped all the essentials out, I start segregating places by date. The app supports multi-day itineraries, of course, so when I look at a cluster of places, I can set a date for them so I know which part of the city I'm going to be in.

While I'd be jumping between my notes and Google Maps earlier on, this self-hosted app presents everything in a single view which makes putting together an itinerary that much easier. Right now, I'm grouping days by area. I've got a day planned out for central Paris, another around my favorite Montmartre district, and a day planned out for shopping. I can then further move locations up and down the list to better align with real-world walking distances, so I'm never too far away from an attraction.

Subscribe to the newsletter for map-first travel planning

Get more by subscribing to the newsletter for focused coverage of map-first travel tools and practical itinerary tactics. Expect in-depth looks at self-hosted planners like Trip, collaboration tips, and organizing methods to turn scattered pins into day-by-day plans.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

If you're planning a trip with a friend or relative, collaboration is also built, and you can shoot across a link that lets other people add in their interests as well. So, if your significant other wants to spend time looking up Parisian boutiques, you have the data right there, and you can slide it right into your existing itinerary.

Built for organizing your travel plans

Trip is far from the only trip-planning tool around. However, most of them tend to focus heavily on recommendations. That might be good for novice travelers, but I tend to do my own research and have a pretty good idea of what I want to do. Trip, on the other hand, gives you no recommendations at all. Instead, it gives you the tools to organize your travel research better, which is precisely what I needed. If that's you, you might want to install this open-source app and give it a look before your next trip.

Trip

Trip is a self-hosted travel planning app that helps you map, organize and structure your itinerary in one place.

See at Github

If I want to pass on that information along, I have to scour through photos to put the pieces back together. With Trip, that's no longer a stress point. It even lets you print out a cleaned-up version of your itinerary.

Turn saved places into a plan that you can follow

Map out your days, not just locations

As I mentioned earlier, saving data points is the easy part. The real complexity arises when you are trying to build a travel plan that you can follow through on when you're on the ground. That's where Trip gets really good. Once I've mapped all the essentials out, I start segregating places by date. The app supports multi-day itineraries, of course, so when I look at a cluster of places, I can set a date for them so I know which part of the city I'm going to be in.

While I'd be jumping between my notes and Google Maps earlier on, this self-hosted app presents everything in a single view which makes putting together an itinerary that much easier. Right now, I'm grouping days by area. I've got a day planned out for central Paris, another around my favorite Montmartre district, and a day planned out for shopping. I can then further move locations up and down the list to better align with real-world walking distances, so I'm never too far away from an attraction.

Subscribe to the newsletter for map-first travel planning

Get more by subscribing to the newsletter for focused coverage of map-first travel tools and practical itinerary tactics. Expect in-depth looks at self-hosted planners like Trip, collaboration tips, and organizing methods to turn scattered pins into day-by-day plans.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

If you're planning a trip with a friend or relative, collaboration is also built, and you can shoot across a link that lets other people add in their interests as well. So, if your significant other wants to spend time looking up Parisian boutiques, you have the data right there, and you can slide it right into your existing itinerary.

Built for organizing your travel plans

Trip is far from the only trip-planning tool around. However, most of them tend to focus heavily on recommendations. That might be good for novice travelers, but I tend to do my own research and have a pretty good idea of what I want to do. Trip, on the other hand, gives you no recommendations at all. Instead, it gives you the tools to organize your travel research better, which is precisely what I needed. If that's you, you might want to install this open-source app and give it a look before your next trip.

Trip

Trip is a self-hosted travel planning app that helps you map, organize and structure your itinerary in one place.

See at Github

item.title: Microsoft finally admitted Windows 11 was broken, but I've already moved on
item.itunes:title: Microsoft finally admitted Windows 11 was broken, but I've already moved on
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Recently, in a unexpected yet long overdue move, Microsoft announced its plans to undergo major changes in Windows 11 in order to regain user trust and deliver the experience fans have been asking for. It's a legitimately great step in the right direction, and it's great to see Microsoft directly address people's feedback for once.

However, for me, it's too little, too late. In the past year or so, I've tried Linux for the first time and had enough time to fall in love with it and learn the ins and outs of using it, and I've also realized that I simply have no interested in going back to Windows. Whether it's Linux or macOS, I'd rather take one of the alternatives over Microsoft's offering.

I'm switching to Linux for gaming — here's why

Linux gaming is finally viable for me.

2
By  Charles Wolfe

Windows is finally addressing its problems

It's about time

Credit: Microsoft / XDA

Let me start by giving Microsoft credit where it's due. It takes some degree of courage to admit that things have been going in the wrong direction and to take steps toward fixing those problems. In announcing the upcoming changes to Windows 11, Windows chief Pavan Davuluri demonstrated a level of openness to feedback that I didn't think Microsoft was capable of anymore, and that was refreshing to see.

With upcoming updates, Windows 11 will finally bring back the ability to move the taskbar to any edge of the screen (something I never particularly cared about, but welcome nonetheless), reducing disruptions caused by updates, removing Copilot and AI integrations from areas where they're not needed, and improving File Explorer reliability. All of these are very welcome improvements that arguably should have happened long ago, and they signal a focus on actually improving the user experience, rather than simply using Windows as a vehicle for whatever high-level executives at Microsoft want to sell.

And that may not even be all of it. Recently, Microsoft's Principal Lead Architect, Rudy Huyn, posted online seeking developers who want to craft "100% native" apps for Windows, suggesting the company may be moving away from the sea of web-based apps that the OS has been shipping with. If it pans out, this would be a very significant course correction for Microsoft.

I’m a power user and I’m disabling these Windows features on purpose

If you've used Windows 11 for any length of time, you'll understand.

3
By  Korbin Brown

How long will this last?

Changes need to be future-proof

All of this is fantastic news, though it doesn't address all the problems with Windows 11, like the forced use of a Microsoft account or features like OneDrive backups being enabled by default during setup. And that's part of the problem here — we still don't know just how much of Windows will be fixed and, if those who left Windows come running back, if the company will just give up on making further changes.

Will Windows have a File Explorer that performs on the level of something like File Pilot? Will dark mode finally be implemented consistently across the UI? If you turn off OneDrive backups, will Microsoft stop showing full-screen reminders about the feature after installing any system updates? It's hard to know if all these little annoyances will be fixed in the near future, if ever.

And on that note, it's also hard to dismiss the idea that this feels like just another step in the never-ending cycle of Windows. Things improve once when Microsoft realizes it's losing customer trust, it wins back their favor, and eventually the experience just degrades again. Windows 10 was the big comeback moment for Windows after the disaster of Windows 8.1. Windows 7 was the same after Vista. Eventually, Microsoft's poor decision-making seems to come back to haunt Windows users, and it's hard to be convinced to come back.

8 essential features Windows 11 is still missing

Microsoft needs to do better

1
By  João Carrasqueira

Linux and macOS have been serving me very well

I don't need to go back

On top of all that, I would be hard-pressed to say I really miss anything about Windows in the first place. For close to a year and a half, my primary desktop computer has been an M4 Mac Mini, and in the last 9 months or so, Linux has also replaced Windows on my primary laptop. And I'm very happy with this situation.

macOS may have an initial setup that's almost as cluttered as that of Windows, but once you get past the initial setup, everything works just fine and the system is far less intrusive about... well, everything. There are no forced backups to iCloud, no random updates that decide to advertise Apple services with full-screen disruptive ads (there was a period where Apple tried to get me to use Apple Intelligence, but it thankfully went away relatively quickly), and the system as a whole is just responsive and fast. It has a search feature that actually works decently well (though I still replaced it with Raycast), and a cohesive design language.

Perhaps most importantly, everything I need to do and use works on macOS. Windows may be known for supporting more software than any other platform, but most of that software isn't really a requirement for a lot of people. I can run my browser, my office suite, my graphics tools, my streaming setup, and my video editing software on macOS completely fine. Many of those things are even better on macOS.

And things aren't all that different on Linux. App support may be a little more spotty, but there are very good alternatives to everything I need. In fact, all the tools I just mentioned are the same on my Mac and my Linux laptop. The browser is Vivaldi, the office suite is OnlyOffice, the graphics workspace is Affinity (which runs on Linux through Wine), my streaming setup is based on OBS, and I recently learned I can run my full video editing workflow in DaVinci Resolve on Linux as well. And it only gets better every time I learn about something new.

Plus, Linux itself is fantastic. I've mostly used Arch Linux, and yes, the initial setup can be a little confusing for a newcomer, but once you know things, Linux is easily the fastest system to set up. No internet requirements during setup, no online accounts, no clutter. I just recently installed CachyOS on another computer, and it took about 30 minutes between starting to download the ISO and getting to the desktop. That's to say nothing of the huge degree of customization that Linux allows you to undertake, so you can make the desktop look like almost anything you want, and it typically feels natural, rather than feeling loosely held by string like many Windows customizations do.

I tried Linux Mint as a lifelong Windows user, and the customization blew me away

Linux Mint is on a whole different level

47
By  João Carrasqueira

Windows will probably be fine (for now)

It's too big to fail... maybe

All the fixes Microsoft is making to Windows may not be enough to convince me to return, but it's hard to deny that it's probably enough for Microsoft to save face. It's something of a repeating cycle, and every time Windows falters, it loses a handful of users to either macOS or Linux, but it's not enough to really drive a major change in the landscape before Microsoft makes enough fixes to win consumers back.

This time does feel a bit different, as Linux has gotten increasingly closer to being a suitable replacement for Windows, especially with major progress being made in gaming thanks in large part to Valve. Other technologies like Flatpak have also made the system more accessible to more users, though it still hasn't been enough to radically change the landscape of desktop computers.

But it feels like this cycle of losing user trust and trying to regain it will eventually cause Windows to lose its stranglehold on the desktop market. I was a Windows diehard for years, and even I have converted. I would say Microsoft needs to get its act together, but I would actually welcome a world where market share is more favorable towards other platforms.

6 reasons you're going to keep using Windows despite its problems

Keep complaining

16
By  João Carrasqueira

item.title: 4 one-click BIOS changes that unlock the PC performance you paid for
item.itunes:title: 4 one-click BIOS changes that unlock the PC performance you paid for
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
The vast majority of people buy or build a PC, boot straight into Windows, and start using it immediately, under the impression that they're getting the performance promised to them by the reviews and specs they researched before purchasing. The truth is, a lot of what allows your PC to run to its full performance potential lies in the BIOS, the firmware that sits beneath your OS.

The BIOS can seem like a scary place to be if you're inexperienced with computers, but the settings within are really integral to your system's performance. The good news is, most of the settings that actually make a difference in performance aren't buried in there, and almost all of them require just one click to toggle on or off. None of the changes covered here require overclocking experience, and none of them involve manually pushing voltages or clock speeds beyond what your hardware is designed to handle. If you haven't touched your BIOS at all since you got your computer, chances are at least one of these settings is sitting at default, and those defaults leave performance on the table.

5 hidden BIOS tweaks to slash your power usage and temperatures

Achieve a cooler system without losing performance

4
By  Tanveer Singh

Enable XMP or EXPO

Even if you think it's already on, you should check it

This is arguably the single most common performance oversight in PC building, and it affects brand-new systems just as often as older ones. When you install a RAM kit rated for DDR5-6000 or DDR4-3600, that speed is not enabled automatically. Out of the box, your motherboard runs memory at a safe, conservative baseline, which is often DDR5-4800 or DDR4-2133, regardless of what you actually paid for. Running your memory at these JEDEC standard speeds can affect performance in both productivity and gaming workloads, especially in CPU-bound cases.

XMP, which stands for Extreme Memory Profile, is Intel's implementation of a standardized speed profile that's baked into the RAM itself. EXPO is AMD's equivalent. Enabling either one simply tells the motherboard to run the memory at the speed and timings the manufacturer intended and tested it at. It is technically an overclocking measure, but it's not turning the dials to anything your hardware wasn't necessarily rated for, it's just taking the chains off.

The location varies by manufacturer. On most boards, it lives under the primary "OC" tab, and often under a section for memory settings. Some manufacturers, like ASUS, place a toggle on the main page, so this change really can be done in a single click. Even if you're certain you already turned it on, it's worth it to go check. A CMOS clear or BIOS update can clear your previous configurations, and that includes XMP/EXPO.

4 reasons you should enable AMD EXPO/Intel XMP on your PC

XMP or EXPO is a one-click boost in RAM performance that you should never ignore

By  Tanveer Singh

Enable Resizable BAR

Important for older systems

By default, your CPU can only access 256MB of your GPU's video memory at a time. That limitation is a holdover from older PC architecture, and on modern hardware it creates a bottleneck that didn't need to exist. Resizable BAR, or ReBAR, removes that limitation entirely, allowing your CPU to access the full pool of VRAM at any given time.

The performance impact varies depending on the game and the GPU, but gains of several frames per second in modern titles are well documented, and some games benefit more than others. It's a free improvement that requires no hardware changes whatsoever, and it's really common to be disabled on older systems.

Along with ReBAR, you'll need to enable a separate setting called "Above 4G Decoding", which will allow your system to allocate more than 4GB of memory for your system to access in the first place. If this isn't enabled, ReBAR can't be enabled, and sometimes the ReBAR setting itself won't even show up without enabling it first.

7 things you might still be doing wrong on your gaming PC

You might be unknowingly hurting your PC's performance

8
By  Tanveer Singh

One-click overclocking

It's worth a try

Credit:  

Most "one-click overclocking" features integrated into motherboards just waste power and don't give a meaningful performance uplift in return, but by default, your system is running with somewhat conservative defaults that protect against instability. There are a couple of settings worth trying that aren't motherboard gimmicks.

On AMD systems, the relevant feature is called Precision Boost Overdrive, or PBO. Enabling it allows the CPU to boost higher and hold those boost clocks longer, governed by the chip's own internal limits rather than an arbitrary board-level restriction. The equivalent setting on modern Intel processors is called Intel Turbo Boost, and specifically on their Core Ultra 200 series, "200S Boost" is a way to increase the speed of communication between components on the CPU itself. Both settings are a bit hit-or-miss when it comes to increasing tangible performance, but it's definitely worth a toggle.

5 reasons why you should set custom fan curves in BIOS

Allow your motherboard to control your fan speeds.

By  Rich Edmonds

Fix your boot settings

You're probably waiting too long for your system to boot

While boot settings don't affect benchmark scores or gaming performance, they have the potential to provide noticeable improvement to the overall experience of actually using your computer.

Unlock PC gains—subscribe to the newsletter for BIOS tips

Subscribe to our newsletter for practical BIOS and PC-optimization guidance—straightforward, tested tweaks with clear walkthroughs that help you unlock more reliable performance from your hardware without risky manual overclocking.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

Fast Boot is a setting that skips some hardware checks in the booting process, allowing your system to go from a powered-down state to desktop in significantly less time. This helps, but there's another key setting on AMD systems that is almost life-changing.

Memory Context Restore is an AMD-specific feature that saves memory training data between boots, allowing your system to skip that step entirely and shaves several seconds off of boot times. In my case, my 64 GB kit of DDR5-6000 took forever to train with every boot, and enabling this feature quite literally cut my boot times by nearly a full minute.

7 tips to improve gaming performance without taking your wallet out

Don't want to spend? No problem. You can boost your FPS for free with these tips

By  Tanveer Singh

The BIOS is worth your time

Four settings, maybe ten minutes of total work, and your PC is running meaningfully closer to what it was always capable of. The gap between a default BIOS and a properly configured one is bigger than most people expect, and the fact that it costs nothing to close that gap makes it one of the best investments of time you can make as a PC owner.

The good news is, most of the settings that actually make a difference in performance aren't buried in there, and almost all of them require just one click to toggle on or off. None of the changes covered here require overclocking experience, and none of them involve manually pushing voltages or clock speeds beyond what your hardware is designed to handle. If you haven't touched your BIOS at all since you got your computer, chances are at least one of these settings is sitting at default, and those defaults leave performance on the table.

5 hidden BIOS tweaks to slash your power usage and temperatures

Achieve a cooler system without losing performance

4
By  Tanveer Singh

Enable XMP or EXPO

Even if you think it's already on, you should check it

This is arguably the single most common performance oversight in PC building, and it affects brand-new systems just as often as older ones. When you install a RAM kit rated for DDR5-6000 or DDR4-3600, that speed is not enabled automatically. Out of the box, your motherboard runs memory at a safe, conservative baseline, which is often DDR5-4800 or DDR4-2133, regardless of what you actually paid for. Running your memory at these JEDEC standard speeds can affect performance in both productivity and gaming workloads, especially in CPU-bound cases.

XMP, which stands for Extreme Memory Profile, is Intel's implementation of a standardized speed profile that's baked into the RAM itself. EXPO is AMD's equivalent. Enabling either one simply tells the motherboard to run the memory at the speed and timings the manufacturer intended and tested it at. It is technically an overclocking measure, but it's not turning the dials to anything your hardware wasn't necessarily rated for, it's just taking the chains off.

The location varies by manufacturer. On most boards, it lives under the primary "OC" tab, and often under a section for memory settings. Some manufacturers, like ASUS, place a toggle on the main page, so this change really can be done in a single click. Even if you're certain you already turned it on, it's worth it to go check. A CMOS clear or BIOS update can clear your previous configurations, and that includes XMP/EXPO.

4 reasons you should enable AMD EXPO/Intel XMP on your PC

XMP or EXPO is a one-click boost in RAM performance that you should never ignore

By  Tanveer Singh

Enable Resizable BAR

Important for older systems

By default, your CPU can only access 256MB of your GPU's video memory at a time. That limitation is a holdover from older PC architecture, and on modern hardware it creates a bottleneck that didn't need to exist. Resizable BAR, or ReBAR, removes that limitation entirely, allowing your CPU to access the full pool of VRAM at any given time.

The performance impact varies depending on the game and the GPU, but gains of several frames per second in modern titles are well documented, and some games benefit more than others. It's a free improvement that requires no hardware changes whatsoever, and it's really common to be disabled on older systems.

Along with ReBAR, you'll need to enable a separate setting called "Above 4G Decoding", which will allow your system to allocate more than 4GB of memory for your system to access in the first place. If this isn't enabled, ReBAR can't be enabled, and sometimes the ReBAR setting itself won't even show up without enabling it first.

7 things you might still be doing wrong on your gaming PC

You might be unknowingly hurting your PC's performance

8
By  Tanveer Singh

One-click overclocking

It's worth a try

Credit:  

Most "one-click overclocking" features integrated into motherboards just waste power and don't give a meaningful performance uplift in return, but by default, your system is running with somewhat conservative defaults that protect against instability. There are a couple of settings worth trying that aren't motherboard gimmicks.

On AMD systems, the relevant feature is called Precision Boost Overdrive, or PBO. Enabling it allows the CPU to boost higher and hold those boost clocks longer, governed by the chip's own internal limits rather than an arbitrary board-level restriction. The equivalent setting on modern Intel processors is called Intel Turbo Boost, and specifically on their Core Ultra 200 series, "200S Boost" is a way to increase the speed of communication between components on the CPU itself. Both settings are a bit hit-or-miss when it comes to increasing tangible performance, but it's definitely worth a toggle.

5 reasons why you should set custom fan curves in BIOS

Allow your motherboard to control your fan speeds.

By  Rich Edmonds

Fix your boot settings

You're probably waiting too long for your system to boot

While boot settings don't affect benchmark scores or gaming performance, they have the potential to provide noticeable improvement to the overall experience of actually using your computer.

Unlock PC gains—subscribe to the newsletter for BIOS tips

Subscribe to our newsletter for practical BIOS and PC-optimization guidance—straightforward, tested tweaks with clear walkthroughs that help you unlock more reliable performance from your hardware without risky manual overclocking.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

Fast Boot is a setting that skips some hardware checks in the booting process, allowing your system to go from a powered-down state to desktop in significantly less time. This helps, but there's another key setting on AMD systems that is almost life-changing.

Memory Context Restore is an AMD-specific feature that saves memory training data between boots, allowing your system to skip that step entirely and shaves several seconds off of boot times. In my case, my 64 GB kit of DDR5-6000 took forever to train with every boot, and enabling this feature quite literally cut my boot times by nearly a full minute.

7 tips to improve gaming performance without taking your wallet out

Don't want to spend? No problem. You can boost your FPS for free with these tips

By  Tanveer Singh

The BIOS is worth your time

Four settings, maybe ten minutes of total work, and your PC is running meaningfully closer to what it was always capable of. The gap between a default BIOS and a properly configured one is bigger than most people expect, and the fact that it costs nothing to close that gap makes it one of the best investments of time you can make as a PC owner.
item.content:encoded:

The truth is, a lot of what allows your PC to run to its full performance potential lies in the BIOS, the firmware that sits beneath your OS.

The BIOS can seem like a scary place to be if you're inexperienced with computers, but the settings within are really integral to your system's performance. The good news is, most of the settings that actually make a difference in performance aren't buried in there, and almost all of them require just one click to toggle on or off. None of the changes covered here require overclocking experience, and none of them involve manually pushing voltages or clock speeds beyond what your hardware is designed to handle. If you haven't touched your BIOS at all since you got your computer, chances are at least one of these settings is sitting at default, and those defaults leave performance on the table.

Achieve a cooler system without losing performance

4
By  Tanveer Singh

Out of the box, your motherboard runs memory at a safe, conservative baseline, which is often DDR5-4800 or DDR4-2133, regardless of what you actually paid for. Running your memory at these JEDEC standard speeds can affect performance in both productivity and gaming workloads, especially in CPU-bound cases.

It is technically an overclocking measure, but it's not turning the dials to anything your hardware wasn't necessarily rated for, it's just taking the chains off.

The location varies by manufacturer. On most boards, it lives under the primary "OC" tab, and often under a section for memory settings. Some manufacturers, like ASUS, place a toggle on the main page, so this change really can be done in a single click. Even if you're certain you already turned it on, it's worth it to go check. A CMOS clear or BIOS update can clear your previous configurations, and that includes XMP/EXPO.

By  Tanveer Singh

If this isn't enabled, ReBAR can't be enabled, and sometimes the ReBAR setting itself won't even show up without enabling it first.

7 things you might still be doing wrong on your gaming PC

You might be unknowingly hurting your PC's performance

8
By  Tanveer Singh

It's worth a try

Credit:  

Most "one-click overclocking" features integrated into motherboards just waste power and don't give a meaningful performance uplift in return, but by default, your system is running with somewhat conservative defaults that protect against instability. On AMD systems, the relevant feature is called Precision Boost Overdrive, or PBO. Enabling it allows the CPU to boost higher and hold those boost clocks longer, governed by the chip's own internal limits rather than an arbitrary board-level restriction. The equivalent setting on modern Intel processors is called Intel Turbo Boost, and specifically on their Core Ultra 200 series, "200S Boost" is a way to increase the speed of communication between components on the CPU itself. Both settings are a bit hit-or-miss when it comes to increasing tangible performance, but it's definitely worth a toggle.

5 reasons why you should set custom fan curves in BIOS

Allow your motherboard to control your fan speeds.

By  Rich Edmonds

You're probably waiting too long for your system to boot

While boot settings don't affect benchmark scores or gaming performance, they have the potential to provide noticeable improvement to the overall experience of actually using your computer.

Unlock PC gains—subscribe to the newsletter for BIOS tips

Subscribe to our newsletter for practical BIOS and PC-optimization guidance—straightforward, tested tweaks with clear walkthroughs that help you unlock more reliable performance from your hardware without risky manual overclocking.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

Fast Boot is a setting that skips some hardware checks in the booting process, allowing your system to go from a powered-down state to desktop in significantly less time. This helps, but there's another key setting on AMD systems that is almost life-changing.

Memory Context Restore is an AMD-specific feature that saves memory training data between boots, allowing your system to skip that step entirely and shaves several seconds off of boot times. In my case, my 64 GB kit of DDR5-6000 took forever to train with every boot, and enabling this feature quite literally cut my boot times by nearly a full minute.

7 tips to improve gaming performance without taking your wallet out

Don't want to spend? No problem. You can boost your FPS for free with these tips

By  Tanveer Singh

The BIOS is worth your time

Four settings, maybe ten minutes of total work, and your PC is running meaningfully closer to what it was always capable of. The gap between a default BIOS and a properly configured one is bigger than most people expect, and the fact that it costs nothing to close that gap makes it one of the best investments of time you can make as a PC owner.

item.title: Most expensive HDMI cables are a waste of money — but this one isn’t
item.itunes:title: Most expensive HDMI cables are a waste of money — but this one isn’t
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Summary

  • Full-fiber HDMI 2.1 uses light, so nearly zero signal loss over long runs.
  • This cable supports HDMI 2.1 and features: 8K/60fps, 4K/120fps, HDR, eARC at 48Gbps.
  • It's thin, flexible, easy to hide; a swappable connector for future HDMI updates.

Expensive HDMI cables, whether they're gold-plated or fiber optic, are often a waste of money and little more than elaborate marketing schemes designed to make you think you need to shell out extra cash for a top-tier, reliable connectivity experience.

However, that isn't the case with this viral, lengthy fiber optic HDMI cable, according to tech enthusiast and content creator Ben Geskin (via Tom's Hardware). In a recent post on X that's almost hit a million views, Geskin shows off the Ruipro 8K, a "full fiber optic armored" HDMI 2.1 cable.

In his post, Geskin notes that "The signal is transmitted with light instead of copper, so there's basically zero signal loss even over long distances." The cable features HDMI 2.1, supporting a variety of resolutions and frame rates, including 8K/60fps and 4K/120fps at 48Gbps, with HDR and eARC.

Don't use old HDMI cables with your new TV or monitor

If you're planning to take advantage of high-end features like 120Hz, HDR, and 4K, it's worth making sure you're using the right HDMI cable.

4
By  Patrick O'Rourke

The longer the HDMI cable, the more problems you can potentially run into

With this fiber HDMI cable, that isn't the case

Credit: Ben Geskin

Geskin goes on to note that because the Ruipro 8K HDMI cable is so thin and flexible, it's easier to hide in a TV or PC setup. As someone a little obsessed with cable management, I definitely find this appealing. It's also interesting that the cable is future-proof, allowing you to swap its tip once HDMI 2.2 becomes more common in a few years, removing the need to buy an entirely new HDMI cable (I didn't realize this was a thing with fiber HDMI cables).

When HDMI cables are long, there tends to be some signal loss, which can cause handshake issues with connected devices, and in some cases, other bandwidth-related problems. With a fiber optic HDMI cable, this isn't a problem, thanks to its use of an optical signal rather than electrical signals to transmit video and audio. Additionally, this type of HDMi cable avoids signal interference, which can occur with some more complicated HDMI setups, especially when you add a switcher to the mix.

Of course, this additional performance and quality comes at a cost. For example, a 3ft cable starts at $117 on Amazon, but the longer the Rupipro 8K cable is, the more cost-effective it becomes. A 15ft cable costs $123, while a 33ft version costs $126. If you're looking to pull together some truly wild wiring, there's a 990ft option that costs $506. The whole point of this HDMI cable is its length, so purchasing a shorter version doesn't make sense anyway.

I don't have a use for a lengthy fiber optic HDMI cable like this right now, but I can see myself picking one up to connect my gaming PC in my basement to my living room TV, if I ever decide that's something I want to do (I'm sure my partner would love that lengthy cable running through our house).

Ruipro 8K Detachable Full Fiber Optic Armored HDMI 2.1 Cable — 33ft
$127 at Amazon
Out of HDMI Ports on your TV? Here's a simple way to add more

HDMI switches have come a long way in the past few years and are often very reliable now.

2
By  Patrick O'Rourke

By  Patrick O'Rourke

item.title: I ditched Claude Code and OpenCode for Pi, and my coding workflow became predictable again
item.itunes:title: I ditched Claude Code and OpenCode for Pi, and my coding workflow became predictable again
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Whatever your feelings about AI-coding are, it's not going away any time soon. Whether you're self-hosting a local LLM or using the heft of cloud-based models, they all work better when used by a harness. That's a software stack that wraps around the LLM, handling tool calls, memory, and file usage. It's not that far off an IDE in practice, and most AI-first coding tools are harnesses, whether it's Claude Code, OpenCode, or my current favorite, Pi.

That's partly because it starts as a very minimal Terminal app, letting you create the harness that your workflow needs. But it's also because it's part of a larger ecosystem for building AI agents and managing LLMs, which I jive with for vibe coding.

I thought vibe-coding was just for developers — Claude proved me wrong

It’s not just a dev thing

5
By  Nolen Jonker

The spectrum of permissibility

It's a fight between locked-down and do-what-you-will

When you're using agentic coding tools, there are so many variables to consider beyond which one looks best. Model support is a huge factor, so is what sort of limitations and guardrails are in place so that your agent doesn't nuke your development repo.

And so is the GUI, TUI, or CLI that will be running your agents. Some prefer the hands-off approach, while others, like Mario Zechner, who created Pi, want to see every aspect of the agent at work.

Pi

OpenCode

Claude Code

Creator

Mario Zechner

Large open-source project, many contributors

Anthropic's own CLI harness for Claude

Primary goal

Minimal, opinionated harness built to Zechner's own demands

Editor-agnostic agent that works in IDEs, terminals, or desktop apps

Low-level harness integrated into Claude's ecosystem

Model support

Muti-provider (Anthropic, OpenAI, Google, xAI, Groq, Cerebras, OpenRouter, OpenAI‑compatible/self‑hosted endpoints)

Over 75 supported providers/models via Models.dev, including locally-hosted ones

Mostly tied to Claude variants, but can be used with other LLMs with a little setup time

Tooling model

Four core tools: read, write, edit, bash (plus optional read-only tools of grep, find, ls)

"Claude-style" tool set, LSP, and MCP integrations

Huge range of built-in tools and structured workflows

Safety stance

YOLO, you're in charge of the keyboard (and the results)

Safer defaults, but option to make it more permissive

Deny-first by default, strong defaults for other guardrails

Integration

Node/TS packages meant to embed into other systems

TUI, desktop app, IDE/ACP plugins

CLI/terminal experience inside Anthropic's ecosystem, desktop app

I've used all three, and can see why each have its own place in the market, but it really depends on which LLM ecosystem you are in already, and how much control over your harness (not just the code it creates).

I have to say that Pi's approach has grown on me

Pi's creator wrote this harness after using mostly Claude Code since Anthropic released it. As Anthropic added more and more functionality, he decided to write a harness to gain control. Not only being able to "inspect every aspect of my interactions with the model," but also controlling the inputs into the model's context.

Did you know that most harnesses inject things into the context window without your consent or knowledge? Some of those are probably important things the harness creator thought needed to be in every prompt, but when you're not in control of any changes, that makes your workflow unpredictable, as you never know when things will change.

  • Pi
    See at Github
  • Claude
    OS
    Windows, macOS
    Individual pricing
    Free plan available; $17/month Pro plan
    See at Official Site
  • OpenCode
    See at Official Site

Why I prefer using Pi

Pi gives me the freedom that other harnesses are lacking

Pi does a few things better than other coding harnesses I've used. For a start, it uses fewer than 1,000 tokens for its system prompt, which describes bash, read, edit, and write as core tools. Fewer tools mean a larger context window for your workload, as you often lose 10k tokens in rules, examples, and safety instructions built into OpenCode or Claude Code.

It's also a difference in methodology. Most harnesses try to solve context for you, while Pi expects you to create a structure with markdown files dedicated to planning, todo, and other aspects that are second-nature in Claude Code and others. It might take a little longer to set up, but it's more reproducible because the LLM gets the same structured files when prompted again.

Pi also shows you code differences as it rewrites them, with a streamed view. That's fantastic for me, because I'm still learning the languages that I use most, and being able to see fixes as they happen is almost as good as being in a lecture hall.

And will willingly self-modify

The other really cool thing about Pi for me is that I can think of an extension that I need to accomplish something, and Pi will create it for me. Yes, that's an LLM harness willingly self-modifying, in a similar way to OpenClaw. One of the existing extensions is permission-gate.ts, which lets you block dangerous commands. I haven't been brave enough to see if it can block rm but maybe it can.

Subscribe for LLM harness insights and tool reviews

The newsletter untangles practical tradeoffs across AI-coding harnesses—context injection, token budgets, tool sets, safety guardrails, and extension workflows. Subscribing delivers focused, comparative coverage to help you evaluate those options.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

They're written in TypeScript, which makes it almost as easy to read as the markdown files other harnesses prefer, but it really doesn't matter because Pi can create them for you. That, combined with being able to connect to multiple thinking models, is a game-changer for me, I know Skills work sort of in the same way, but being able to build the specific tools I need is awesome.

I turned my ROG Ally into a ROG Enemy with this one simple trick and a lot of Claude

One bold move turned a gaming handheld into a powerful AI assistant, but at what cost?

1
By  Joe Rice-Jones

Pi is closer to the terminal coding assistant I want to use

I like being in control, but I also like that Pi drastically reduces the number of tokens of context fed from a prompt. That means I get more thinking for my bucks, and that's a fantastic feeling. But I also like that it's minimal and predictable, while still allowing extensions to add workflow-specific parts.

Safety stance

YOLO, you're in charge of the keyboard (and the results)

Safer defaults, but option to make it more permissive

Deny-first by default, strong defaults for other guardrails

Integration

Node/TS packages meant to embed into other systems

TUI, desktop app, IDE/ACP plugins

CLI/terminal experience inside Anthropic's ecosystem, desktop app

I've used all three, and can see why each have its own place in the market, but it really depends on which LLM ecosystem you are in already, and how much control over your harness (not just the code it creates).

I have to say that Pi's approach has grown on me

Pi's creator wrote this harness after using mostly Claude Code since Anthropic released it. As Anthropic added more and more functionality, he decided to write a harness to gain control. Not only being able to "inspect every aspect of my interactions with the model," but also controlling the inputs into the model's context.

Did you know that most harnesses inject things into the context window without your consent or knowledge? Some of those are probably important things the harness creator thought needed to be in every prompt, but when you're not in control of any changes, that makes your workflow unpredictable, as you never know when things will change.

  • Pi
    See at Github
  • Claude
    OS
    Windows, macOS
    Individual pricing
    Free plan available; $17/month Pro plan
    See at Official Site
  • OpenCode
    See at Official Site

Why I prefer using Pi

Pi gives me the freedom that other harnesses are lacking

Pi does a few things better than other coding harnesses I've used. For a start, it uses fewer than 1,000 tokens for its system prompt, which describes bash, read, edit, and write as core tools. Fewer tools mean a larger context window for your workload, as you often lose 10k tokens in rules, examples, and safety instructions built into OpenCode or Claude Code.

It's also a difference in methodology. Most harnesses try to solve context for you, while Pi expects you to create a structure with markdown files dedicated to planning, todo, and other aspects that are second-nature in Claude Code and others. It might take a little longer to set up, but it's more reproducible because the LLM gets the same structured files when prompted again.

Pi also shows you code differences as it rewrites them, with a streamed view. That's fantastic for me, because I'm still learning the languages that I use most, and being able to see fixes as they happen is almost as good as being in a lecture hall.

And will willingly self-modify

The other really cool thing about Pi for me is that I can think of an extension that I need to accomplish something, and Pi will create it for me. Yes, that's an LLM harness willingly self-modifying, in a similar way to OpenClaw. One of the existing extensions is permission-gate.ts, which lets you block dangerous commands. I haven't been brave enough to see if it can block rm but maybe it can.

Subscribe for LLM harness insights and tool reviews

The newsletter untangles practical tradeoffs across AI-coding harnesses—context injection, token budgets, tool sets, safety guardrails, and extension workflows. Subscribing delivers focused, comparative coverage to help you evaluate those options.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

They're written in TypeScript, which makes it almost as easy to read as the markdown files other harnesses prefer, but it really doesn't matter because Pi can create them for you. That, combined with being able to connect to multiple thinking models, is a game-changer for me, I know Skills work sort of in the same way, but being able to build the specific tools I need is awesome.

I turned my ROG Ally into a ROG Enemy with this one simple trick and a lot of Claude

One bold move turned a gaming handheld into a powerful AI assistant, but at what cost?

1
By  Joe Rice-Jones

Pi is closer to the terminal coding assistant I want to use

I like being in control, but I also like that Pi drastically reduces the number of tokens of context fed from a prompt. That means I get more thinking for my bucks, and that's a fantastic feeling. But I also like that it's minimal and predictable, while still allowing extensions to add workflow-specific parts.
item.content:encoded:
Whatever your feelings about AI-coding are, it's not going away any time soon. Whether you're self-hosting a local LLM or using the heft of cloud-based models, they all work better when used by a harness. That's a software stack that wraps around the LLM, handling tool calls, memory, and file usage. It's not that far off an IDE in practice, and most AI-first coding tools are harnesses, whether it's Claude Code, OpenCode, or my current favorite, Pi.

That's partly because it starts as a very minimal Terminal app, letting you create the harness that your workflow needs. But it's also because it's part of a larger ecosystem for building AI agents and managing LLMs, which I jive with for vibe coding.

I thought vibe-coding was just for developers — Claude proved me wrong

It’s not just a dev thing

5
By  Nolen Jonker

The spectrum of permissibility

It's a fight between locked-down and do-what-you-will

When you're using agentic coding tools, there are so many variables to consider beyond which one looks best. Model support is a huge factor, so is what sort of limitations and guardrails are in place so that your agent doesn't nuke your development repo.

And so is the GUI, TUI, or CLI that will be running your agents. Some prefer the hands-off approach, while others, like Mario Zechner, who created Pi, want to see every aspect of the agent at work.

Pi

OpenCode

Claude Code

Creator

Mario Zechner

Large open-source project, many contributors

Anthropic's own CLI harness for Claude

Primary goal

Minimal, opinionated harness built to Zechner's own demands

Editor-agnostic agent that works in IDEs, terminals, or desktop apps

Low-level harness integrated into Claude's ecosystem

Model support

Muti-provider (Anthropic, OpenAI, Google, xAI, Groq, Cerebras, OpenRouter, OpenAI‑compatible/self‑hosted endpoints)

Over 75 supported providers/models via Models.dev, including locally-hosted ones

Mostly tied to Claude variants, but can be used with other LLMs with a little setup time

Tooling model

Four core tools: read, write, edit, bash (plus optional read-only tools of grep, find, ls)

"Claude-style" tool set, LSP, and MCP integrations

Huge range of built-in tools and structured workflows

Safety stance

YOLO, you're in charge of the keyboard (and the results)

Safer defaults, but option to make it more permissive

Deny-first by default, strong defaults for other guardrails

Integration

Node/TS packages meant to embed into other systems

TUI, desktop app, IDE/ACP plugins

CLI/terminal experience inside Anthropic's ecosystem, desktop app

I've used all three, and can see why each have its own place in the market, but it really depends on which LLM ecosystem you are in already, and how much control over your harness (not just the code it creates).

Not only being able to "inspect every aspect of my interactions with the model," but also controlling the inputs into the model's context.

Did you know that most harnesses inject things into the context window without your consent or knowledge? Some of those are probably important things the harness creator thought needed to be in every prompt, but when you're not in control of any changes, that makes your workflow unpredictable, as you never know when things will change.

    See at Github
  • Claude
    OS

    See at Official Site
  • OpenCode

item.title: A used office PC is the best media center upgrade I've made in years
item.itunes:title: A used office PC is the best media center upgrade I've made in years
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
I’ve spent a long time trying to build the perfect media center at home. I wanted a setup that lets me access everything on my TV without compromises. In practice, it never really worked that way. For a while, I used an old laptop as my media center, and in many ways, it was the closest I got.

It had a capable AMD processor, enough RAM, and proper storage, which already put it ahead of most smart TVs that run on under powered chipsets with limited memory. Performance wasn’t the issue. The problem was reliability. A laptop is not meant to stay plugged in and running all the time, and dealing with battery drain and power management quickly becomes frustrating.

So I moved to a NAS, which solved that part of the problem. It’s designed to stay on, always connected, always available. That makes a big difference for a media setup. But then I ran into a different limitation. My NAS, a Ugreen unit with a low-end Rockchip processor, simply didn’t have the performance headroom. Over time, it started to feel slow, and with soldered RAM, there was no real upgrade path.

That’s when I switched to a used office PC, and it ended up solving both problems at once. It runs like a NAS but delivers the kind of performance you expect from a proper computer. You get more RAM, a better processor, and most importantly, the flexibility to upgrade storage and components over time. It’s easily the most complete media center setup I’ve used so far.

This tiny and cheap x86 mini PC is the best Jellyfin server you're overlooking

Say goodbye to underpowered SBCs and noisy desktops. There's a new contender in town for your Jellyfin server.

2
By  Jeff Butts

Performance is where everything finally clicks

Why a used office PC fixes what most media setups struggle with

Performance is the first thing you notice when you switch to a used office PC, and it changes how your entire media setup behaves day to day. With my earlier setup, most of my library lived on Jellyfin, and that’s where the cracks started to show. A NAS, especially one running a low-end chip, often struggles with transcoding, which is when your server converts a video into a format your device can play. When the hardware isn’t strong enough, two things happen – playback starts buffering, or the server drops quality to keep things running.

A PC changes that completely. Even older Intel chips, especially 6th-gen and above, come with Quick Sync, which handles hardware-accelerated video encoding and decoding. That means Jellyfin can transcode smoothly when needed, or avoid transcoding altogether if your client supports direct play. In both cases, playback is faster, sharper, and far more consistent.

I started noticing more obvious differences after enabling remote access. My NAS has always struggled with it because it simply doesn’t have the performance headroom. Whenever I opened Jellyfin or any self-hosted service from my phone, I knew I was about to sit through loading screens, buffering, or sometimes just give up. The hardware just couldn’t handle multiple requests, background processes, and network overhead at the same time.

On a PC, you can run Jellyfin, a photo server, and other self-hosted tools simultaneously without the system slowing down. Remote access is also usable rather than barely functional, which means pages load faster, streams start sooner, and you’re not constantly adjusting quality settings just to make things work.

It becomes more than just a media setup

A proper PC turns it into a system you can actually build on

Once you move to a used office PC, you stop thinking of your setup as just something that plays content. It becomes a system you can build on and expand over time. A big part of that comes down to how flexible a full PC really is. With something like a NAS, you’re mostly working within the limits of what the software allows. You can install a few apps, maybe tweak a few settings, but that’s about it. On a PC, you’re not boxed in like that.

Subscribe for Practical Home Media Center Guides

Secure deeper how-tos by subscribing to the newsletter: detailed guides on repurposing office PCs, Jellyfin and transcoding tips, hardware recommendations, and upgrade strategies for better home media centers.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

You can run Jellyfin as your main media server, but you’re not limited to just that. If you want to spin up Kodi, you can do that without worrying about the system slowing down. Docker support alone opens up many possibilities. You can host your own apps, experiment with tools, and keep adding functionality without needing separate hardware.

The operating system also gives you more control. If you’re comfortable with Windows, you can stick with it and get a familiar experience that works right out of the box. Everything from browsers to streaming apps to Steam just works. If you want more control, you can switch to Linux and run things in a more modular way, especially if you’re planning to self-host more services.

Another advantage is that this setup doesn’t lock you into a single use case. You can use it as a media center and as a light gaming machine. Even a basic office PC can handle indie titles, older games, and emulation without much effort. That’s something a NAS or streaming device simply can’t do. Then there’s storage. Instead of being limited by fixed bays or soldered components, you can add more drives whenever you need to. As your media library grows, your system grows with it. You’re not forced to replace the entire setup just because you ran out of space.

5 of the best upgrades I’ve made to my custom NAS server

Endless upgradability is my favorite aspect of owning a self-assembled NAS

2
By  Ayush Pande
